summary: The Gemara establishes that Yom Kippur's drinking prohibition applies to all liquids (via gezerah shavah from nazir), discusses the meaning of tirosh (new wine) as a word that spells either wealth or poverty, and derives the sources for bathing and anointing as Yom Kippur afflictions from Scriptural verses.

display.whats: Tirosh (new wine) and the YK drinking prohibition scope
display.hint: Does the Yom Kippur prohibition apply to all beverages or only wine?
display.title: Sheikhar: Linking Yom Kippur to the Nazir

learning.ahaMoment: The word 'sheikhar' in the YK verse is a hook, not a scope limit - it exists to enable the gezerah shavah, not to restrict the prohibition.
learning.memoryAnchor: Sheikhar appears in both places: the shared word imports the nazir's full prohibition into YK.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the Yom Kippur verse use the word 'sheikhar' (strong drink) if the prohibition covers all liquids?
learning.coreTension: Between the literal scope of 'sheikhar' (strong drink only) and the gezerah shavah that extends the prohibition to all drinking via the nazir's prohibition.
learning.coreMove: The shared word 'sheikhar' in the YK verse and the nazir section creates a gezerah shavah that imports the nazir's total prohibition on all drink into YK, expanding far beyond the literal meaning of sheikhar.

takeaway.text: A gezerah shavah uses a shared word as a legal hook to import the full framework of one context into another; the word need not define the scope of the prohibition - it only needs to anchor the analogy.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may think 'sheikhar' limits the prohibition to strong drink; the gezerah shavah mechanism is what matters, not the word's literal meaning.

argumentFlow.label: Liable for drinking on YK
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'Sheikhar' in the verse establishes a drinking prohibition

argumentFlow.label: Gezerah shavah from nazir
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Tirosh = wine on Yom Kippur as for the nazir

argumentFlow.label: Tirosh = new wine, not fully fermented
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: Vow from tirosh: only new wine forbidden

argumentFlow.label: What comes from tirosh is also tirosh
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The wine produced from new wine grapes is also tirosh

argumentFlow.label: Tirosh used for wine in general
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'Harlotry, wine, and tirosh' - tirosh = wine in general usage

argumentFlow.label: Yayin = woe; tirosh = wealth or poverty
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Wine causes woe (vai); tirosh brings wealth if merited, poverty if not

argumentFlow.label: Tirash vs tirosh
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Kahana
argumentFlow.text: Written without vav (tirash=impoverish); read with vav (tirosh=inherit)

halachic: YK drinking prohibition applies to all beverages via gezerah shavah from nazir
tirosh = same substance as wine, different name reflects merit/demerit

quizSeeds.question: Why is the new wine called 'tirosh' in some verses and 'yayin' in others?
quizSeeds.answer: They refer to the same substance: yayin means it causes woe; tirosh means inheritance if used meritoriously, poverty if not

sugyot.finalRuling: YK drinking prohibition extends to all beverages; tirosh = wine, with its dual potential for wealth or poverty.

display.whats: Sources for bathing and anointing as Yom Kippur afflictions
display.hint: From which verses do we derive that bathing and anointing are forbidden on Yom Kippur?
display.title: Anointing as Affliction: Daniel's Proof

learning.ahaMoment: Daniel's mourning list is the Gemara's key: by grouping anointing with fasting from food, it reclassifies anointing from luxury to genuine bodily need.
learning.memoryAnchor: Daniel abstained from bread, water, and anointing - three items, one act of affliction, anointing elevated to need.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the Gemara need multiple proofs to establish that abstaining from anointing counts as inui (affliction)?
learning.coreTension: Between the intuition that anointing is a luxury (not a need, so abstaining from it should not count as affliction) and the halakhic requirement that it does count as inui on Yom Kippur.
learning.coreMove: Daniel's text groups bread, water, and anointing together as what he abstained from in mourning, treating all three as equivalent forms of deprivation. This grouping elevates anointing from luxury to genuine need.

takeaway.text: When a verse groups multiple abstentions together as a single act of self-deprivation, each item in the group gains the halakhic status of affliction, even items that seem like luxuries.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may ask why one clear proof isn't enough; the Gemara needs multiple proofs because bathing and anointing seem like luxuries rather than necessities, making each step of the proof work harder.

argumentFlow.label: Anointing derived from Daniel
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Daniel abstained from bread, water, and anointing - inuy

argumentFlow.label: Bathing derived from same Daniel verse
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Zutra
argumentFlow.text: Bathing implied alongside anointing in Daniel's abstention

argumentFlow.label: Tanna derives anointing from mourner
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: A mourner's anointing prohibition is the source for YK

argumentFlow.label: 'I came according to your words': 70 elders
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The angel's statement relates to the verse about the 70 elders

halachic: bathing and anointing are Yom Kippur prohibitions derived from Daniel's self-affliction and mourner laws
theological: Daniel's voluntary affliction became a halakhic model for Yom Kippur prohibitions

quizSeeds.question: From which verse is anointing derived as a Yom Kippur prohibition?
quizSeeds.answer: From Daniel's self-affliction: 'bread of affliction I did not eat, water I did not drink, and anointing I did not anoint' - inuy covers all three

sugyot.finalRuling: Bathing and anointing are Yom Kippur prohibitions; anointing derived from Daniel; bathing from the same verse or from the mourner parallel.

glossary.en: New wine (grape juice at various stages of fermentation); the Gemara identifies it with wine generally.
A person who has taken a Nazirite vow, abstaining from wine, haircuts, and contact with the dead.
Anointing the body with oil; one of the five activities forbidden on Yom Kippur.

rashiTranslations.he: חייב. משום שכר אל תשת (ויקרא י) : אף כאן יין. וביין אין שכרות
rashiTranslations.en: liable for sheikhar prohibition - but does sheikhar forbid all liquids? Gezerah shavah from nazir: tirosh = wine

rashiTranslations.he: אלא בשתייתו: אסור בכל מיני מתיקה. בפירות חדשים בתפוחים
rashiTranslations.en: it is derived from nazir - just as for the nazir tirosh means wine, so too on Yom Kippur

rashiTranslations.he: בענבים: ומותר ביין. בן ארבעים : ותירוש ינובב בתולות. זה יין
rashiTranslations.en: is tirosh wine? Isn't it taught: one who vows from tirosh is forbidden only from new wine?

rashiTranslations.he: טוב שהוא משכר את האדם לגלות סתרי לבו האטומים כבתולה:
rashiTranslations.en: but isn't it written 'your wine-vats shall overflow with tirosh'? What comes from the tirosh is also called tirosh

rashiTranslations.he: הבא מן התירוש. מן הענבים והכי
rashiTranslations.en: and isn't it written 'harlotry, wine, and tirosh take away the heart'? In general tirosh is the same as wine

rashiTranslations.he: קאמר קרא הענבים יוציאו לכם דבר
rashiTranslations.en: why is it called wine (yayin) and why is it called tirosh (new wine)? Wine = it causes woe (vai); new wine = if you merit, wealth; if not, poverty
Rav Kahana: written tirash but read tirosh - if you merit it becomes an inheritance (tirosh); if not, it impoverishes you (tirash)

rashiTranslations.he: יפרוצו. וכל יקב שבמקרא הוא הבור
rashiTranslations.en: from where do we derive that bathing and anointing are called affliction?

rashiTranslations.he: שלפני הגת ושם אין הענבים נתונים
rashiTranslations.en: from where do we know that anointing is considered affliction? From Daniel - 'bread of affliction I did not eat'

rashiTranslations.he: אלא היין: דבר הבא מתירוש וכו'.
rashiTranslations.en: we have found anointing. From where do we know bathing?

rashiTranslations.he: והכי קאמר קרא ענבים יהיו לך
rashiTranslations.en: but the tanna derives it the other way: from where do we derive that anointing is like affliction - it was taught in the mishnah about a mourner
what does 'I came according to your words' mean? That is what is written: seventy men of the elders of Israel
[Rashi commentary on line 13]: היין נקרא תירוש אלא הענבים:

rashiTranslations.he: והכתיב יין ותירוש יקח לב. ואכילת
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 14]: והכתיב יין ותירוש יקח לב. ואכילת
[Rashi commentary on line 15]: ענבים אינה משכרת: הלך אחר

rashiTranslations.he: לשון בני אדם. ואין דרכן לקרות
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 16]: לשון בני אדם. ואין דרכן לקרות

rashiTranslations.he: את היין תירוש לכך מותר ביין:
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 17]: את היין תירוש לכך מותר ביין:

rashiTranslations.he: שמביא יללה. ע"י היין רב הניאוף
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 18]: שמביא יללה. ע"י היין רב הניאוף
[Rashi commentary on line 19]: ופורענות באה לעולם. יין לשון
[Rashi commentary on line 20]: תאניה ויללה: זכה. לשתות לפי
[Rashi commentary on line 21]: מדה: נעשה ראש. שמפקח לבו
[Rashi commentary on line 22]: בחכמה: כתיב ישמח. בשי"ן לשון

rashiTranslations.he: שממה וקרינן יין ישמח לשון שמחה:
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 23]: שממה וקרינן יין ישמח לשון שמחה:
[Rashi commentary on line 24]: פקחין. פקחוני עשאוני פקח:
[Rashi commentary on line 25]: דחיטי דכייתא. חטים נאות ונקיות
[Rashi commentary on line 26]: כדמתרגמינן את בגדי עשו החמודות
[Rashi commentary on line 27]: (בראשית כז) דכייתא: דומיא דשמן.
[Rashi commentary on line 28]: מים בקרבו דכתיבי בקרא דומיא
[Rashi commentary on line 29]: דכשמן בעצמותיו מה שמן מבחוץ
[Rashi commentary on line 30]: סכין אותו ונבלע בעצמותיו אף
[Rashi commentary on line 31]: מים בקרבו הנבלעות דרך החוץ
[Rashi commentary on line 32]: על ידי רחיצה: שהיא כשתיה.
[Rashi commentary on line 33]: אלמא מים דקרא שתיה הוא

rashiTranslations.he: ושמן הוא דיליף ממים ואת ילפת
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 34]: ושמן הוא דיליף ממים ואת ילפת

rashiTranslations.he: מים משמן: אלא אמר רב אשי
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 35]: מים משמן: אלא אמר רב אשי
[Rashi commentary on line 36]: רחיצה מגופיה דקרא. דדניאל דגבי

rashiTranslations.he: סיכה נפקא לן מריבויא דלישנא וסוך
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 37]: סיכה נפקא לן מריבויא דלישנא וסוך

rashiTranslations.he: לא סכתי ומצי למכתב ולא סכתי:
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 38]: לא סכתי ומצי למכתב ולא סכתי:
[Rashi commentary on line 39]: ואני באתי בדבריך. סיפיה דההוא

rashiTranslations.he: קרא דלעיל אל תירא דניאל וגו'
rashiTranslations.en: [Rashi commentary on line 40]: קרא דלעיל אל תירא דניאל וגו'
[Rashi commentary on line 41]: ואני באתי בדבריך נכנסתי לתוך
[Rashi commentary on line 42]: הפרגוד בשבילך: מאי ואני באתי.
[Rashi commentary on line 43]: מתי נגרש מתוכו שהוצרך ליכנס:
[Rashi commentary on line 44]: לבוש
